391	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
392	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
393	
394	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
395	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
396	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
397	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
398	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
399	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
400	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
401	
402	\begin{lemma}\leanok
403	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
404	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
406	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
407	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
408	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	\begin{proof}
412	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
413	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
414	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
415	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
416	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\begin{lemma}\leanok
420	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
421	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
423	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
424	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
425	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
426	  \[
427	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
428	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
429	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
430	  \]
431	\end{lemma}
432	
433	\begin{proof}
434	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
435	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
436	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
437	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
438	\end{proof}
439	
440	\begin{lemma}
441	[Free-pullback comparison is natural in the base morphism]
442	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
444	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
445	  Let \(\varphi, \psi : T' \to T\) be morphisms with \(\varphi = \psi\) and \(I\) an index
446	  type. The two free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(\varphi\) and
447	  \(\psi\) agree after transporting the differing pullback sources by the canonical
448	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\): the composite of that transport with
449	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)\) equals \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)\).
450	\end{lemma}
451	\begin{proof}
452	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
453	  Substituting the equality \(\varphi = \psi\) collapses the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) to the
454	  identity and the two comparisons coincide.
455	\end{proof}
456	
457	\begin{lemma}
458	[Iso-level free-pullback cancellation]
459	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
460	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
461	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
462	  For equal base morphisms \(\varphi = \psi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), the composite
463	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)^{-1}\) is the
464	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) transport between the (differing) pullback sources of the free sheaf
465	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\). Stated and proved generically in the base morphisms, so its
466	  use never forces evaluation of a concrete immersion; this is the load-bearing replacement
467	  for the cast chain in the self-identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}.
468	\end{lemma}
469	\begin{proof}
470	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
471	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) turns the composite into
472	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)^{-1} =
473	  \mathrm{id}\), which is the \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) of the now-trivial source equality.
474	\end{proof}
475	
476	\begin{lemma}\leanok
477	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
478	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
480	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
481	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
482	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
483	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
484	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
485	\end{lemma}
486	
487	\begin{proof}
488	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
489	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
490	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
491	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
492	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
493	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
494	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
495	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
496	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
497	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
498	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
499	\end{proof}
500	
501	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
502	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
503	
504	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
505	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
506	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
507	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
508	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
509	
510	\begin{definition}\leanok
511	[Global section of the unit module]
512	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
513	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
514	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
515	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
516	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
517	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
518	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
519	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
520	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
521	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
522	  global function.
523	\end{definition}
524	
525	\begin{definition}\leanok
526	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
527	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
528	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
529	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
530	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
531	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
532	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
533	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
534	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
535	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
536	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
537	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
538	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
539	\end{definition}
540	
541	\begin{lemma}\leanok
542	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
543	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
544	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
545	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
546	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
547	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
548	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
549	\end{lemma}
550	
551	\begin{proof}
552	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
553	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
554	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
555	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
556	\end{proof}
557	
558	\begin{lemma}\leanok
559	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
560	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
562	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
563	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
564	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
565	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
570	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
571	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
572	\end{proof}
573	
574	\begin{lemma}\leanok
575	[Composition of scalar endomorphisms is multiplication]
576	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_comp}
578	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
579	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the scalar endomorphisms compose by
580	  multiplication: \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) \circ \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) =
581	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a\,b)\).
582	\end{lemma}
583	\begin{proof}
584	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
585	  Under the identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the
586	  scalar endomorphisms act as multiplication by \(a\) and by \(b\); their composite
587	  is multiplication by \(a\,b\), because the section ring is commutative and
588	  restriction maps are ring homomorphisms.
589	\end{proof}
590	
591	\begin{lemma}\leanok
592	[Scalar endomorphism is additive]
593	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_add}
595	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
596	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) one has \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a + b) =
597	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) + \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b)\).
598	\end{lemma}
599	\begin{proof}
600	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
601	  Multiplication by \(a + b\) equals multiplication by \(a\) plus multiplication by
602	  \(b\) on every section, since the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) are additive;
603	  under \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) this is the stated additivity.
604	\end{proof}
605	
606	\begin{lemma}
607	[Scalar endomorphism of a finite sum]
608	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_sum}
610	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
611	  For a finite index set \(s\) and a family \(f : \iota \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\),
612	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_{i \in s} f_i\bigr) = \sum_{i \in s} \mathrm{scalarEnd}(f_i)\).
613	\end{lemma}
614	\begin{proof}
615	  \uses{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
616	  Induction on the finite set \(s\): the empty case is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
617	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}) and the insertion step is additivity
618	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}).
619	\end{proof}
620	
621	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
622	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
623	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
624	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
625	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
626	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
627	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
628	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
629	
630	\begin{definition}\leanok
631	[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
632	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
634	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
635	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
636	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
637	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
638	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
639	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
640	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
641	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
642	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
643	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
644	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
645	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
646	\end{definition}
647	
648	\begin{lemma}\leanok
649	[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
650	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
651	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
652	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
653	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
654	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
655	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
656	  \[
657	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
658	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
659	  \]
660	\end{lemma}
661	\begin{proof}
662	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
663	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
664	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
665	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
666	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
667	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
668	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
669	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
670	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
671	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
672	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
673	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
674	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
675	\end{proof}
676	
677	\begin{lemma}\leanok
678	[Matrix endomorphism of the identity is the identity]
679	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
680	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_one}
681	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
682	  The matrix endomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism of
683	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\): \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1_{d\times d}) =
684	  \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
685	\end{lemma}
686	\begin{proof}
687	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
688	  The diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\)
689	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the off-diagonal components are
690	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the morphism with this
691	  component matrix is the identity of the biproduct.
692	\end{proof}
693	
694	\begin{definition}\leanok
695	[Free-sheaf automorphism of an invertible matrix]
696	  \label{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}
697	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso}
698	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
699	  Let \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) be mutually
700	  inverse, \(M N = 1\) and \(N M = 1\). The \emph{matrix automorphism}
701	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is the isomorphism of free sheaves
702	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) with forward map
703	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) and inverse map \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)\); the two
704	  composites are identities by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp} (turning the products
705	  \(NM\) and \(MN\) into the relevant compositions) and \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
706	  Its forward map is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) by construction
707	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}).
708	\end{definition}
709	
710	\begin{lemma}\leanok
711	[Forward map of the matrix automorphism]
712	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}
713	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_hom}
714	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
715	  The forward map of \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
716	\end{lemma}
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
719	  Immediate from the definition \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}, whose forward field is
720	  literally \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
721	\end{proof}
722	
723	\begin{lemma}[Matrix automorphisms compose multiplicatively]
724	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul}
725	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_mul}
726	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
727	  Let \(A, A', B, B'\) be \(d \times d\) matrices of global functions on \(S\) with
728	  \(A A' = A' A = 1\), \(B B' = B' B = 1\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')\)
729	  and \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')\) are automorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\).
730	  Their forward maps compose to the forward map of the product automorphism, with the
731	  order reversed by the component contravariance:
732	  \[
733	    \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')_{\mathrm{hom}}
734	      \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')_{\mathrm{hom}}
735	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B \cdot A).
736	  \]
737	  Equivalently, composition of matrix automorphisms realises matrix multiplication of
738	  the underlying matrices. This is the linkage that turns the matrix-level cocycle
739	  identity into a composition identity of sheaf-of-module isomorphisms.
740	\end{lemma}
741	\begin{proof}
742	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
743	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom} the two forward maps are
744	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\) and \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B)\); their composite is
745	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B) = \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B\cdot A)\) by
746	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}.
747	\end{proof}
748	
749	\begin{definition}
750	
751	\leanok
752	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
753	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
755	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
756	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
757	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
758	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
759	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
760	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
761	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
762	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
763	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
764	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
765	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
766	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
767	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
768	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
769	  \[
770	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
771	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
772	  \]
773	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
774	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
775	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
776	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
777	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
778	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
779	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
780	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
781	
782	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
783	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
784	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
785	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
786	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
787	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
788	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
789	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
790	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
791	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
792	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
793	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
794	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
795	  free sheaves'' primitive.
796	\end{definition}
797	
798	\begin{lemma}\leanok
799	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
800	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
801	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
802	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
803	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
804	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
805	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
806	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
807	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
808	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
809	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
810	  identity:
811	  \[
812	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
813	  \]
814	\end{lemma}
815	
816	\begin{proof}
817	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
818	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
819	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
820	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
821	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
822	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
823	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
824	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
825	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
826	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
827	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
828	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
829	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
830	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
831	\end{proof}
832	
833	\begin{lemma}\leanok
834	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
835	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
836	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
837	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
838	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
839	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
840	\end{lemma}
841	
842	\begin{proof}
843	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
844	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
845	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
846	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
847	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
848	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
849	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
850	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
851	  \[
852	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
853	  \]
854	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
855	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
856	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
857	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
858	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
859	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
860	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
861	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
862	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
863	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
864	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
865	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
866	\end{proof}
867	
868	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
869	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
870	
871	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
872	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
873	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
874	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
875	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
876	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
877	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
878	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
879	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
880	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
881	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
882	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.
883	
884	\begin{lemma}
885	[Self-minor Cramer inverse is the identity]
886	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_self}
887	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_self}
888	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
889	  The Cramer inverse of the self-minor is the identity matrix:
890	  \((X^I_I)^{-1} = 1_{d \times d}\).
891	\end{lemma}
892	\begin{proof}
893	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
894	  The self-minor \(X^I_I\) is the identity block
895	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), and \(1^{-1} = 1\).
896	\end{proof}
897	
898	\begin{definition}\leanok
899	[The bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\)]
900	  \label{def:gr_bundleTransition}
901	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition}
902	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
903	    lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_transition}
904	  Let \(I, J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) be size-\(d\) subsets and let
905	  \(f_{IJ} : U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) and \(t_{IJ} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\) be
906	  the overlap immersion and transition of the chart cover (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The
907	  \emph{bundle transition} \(g_{I,J}\) is the isomorphism of sheaves of modules on the
908	  overlap \(U^I_J\)
909	  \[
910	    g_{I,J} \;:\;
911	      f_{IJ}^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\bigr)
912	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
913	      (t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI})^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^J}^{\,d}\bigr)
914	  \]
915	  induced by the invertible \(d \times d\) matrix
916	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J)\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
917	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor, which is invertible because
918	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}).
919	
920	  \emph{Realisation.} The transition is built exactly as the chart quotient
921	  \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}: each entry \((X^I_J)^{-1}_{p,q} \in R^I_J\) is a global
922	  function on the overlap, its scalar endomorphism
923	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}_{p,q}\bigr)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the
924	  \((p,q)\)-component, and the universal property of the rank-\(d\) biproduct assembles
925	  these into an automorphism of the free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\); the
926	  source and target pullbacks of free sheaves are identified with this
927	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\) through the free-pullback comparisons
928	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(f_{IJ}\) and \(t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI}\), conjugating
929	  the matrix automorphism into the displayed isomorphism. Invertibility of the matrix makes
930	  \(g_{I,J}\) an isomorphism.
931	\end{definition}
932

942	  condition (C1) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\).
943	\end{lemma}

948	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), so its Cramer inverse is again the
949	  identity, \((X^I_I)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}).
950	  Hence in the biproduct presentation of \cref{def:gr_bundleTransition} the diagonal
951	  components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
300	
301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}

307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,
310	  invertible because the right adjoint forgetful functor to presheaves of modules
311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}

318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,

321	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
322	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
323	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of
324	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} and composing with the counit

326	  (\(m = 0\)) of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{definition}\leanok
330	[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor

338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor

343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
349	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
350	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
351	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
352	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
353	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
354	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}

358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
366	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
368	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
369	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
370	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  \leanok
380	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
381	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
382	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
383	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
384	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
385	  equivalence.
386	\end{proof}
387	
388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
389	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
390	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
392	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
393	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
394	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
395	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
396	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
397	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
398	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
399	\end{lemma}
400	
401	\begin{proof}
402	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
403	  \leanok
404	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
405	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
406	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
407	\end{proof}

410	[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
411	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
413	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
414	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
415	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
416	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of

418	\end{lemma}

423	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
424	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
425	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.

428	\begin{definition}\leanok
429	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf]
430	  \label{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorDomainPresheaf}
432	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
433	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
434	  assignment \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\),
435	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the underlying restriction
436	  maps, is a functor \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is
437	  the codomain row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
438	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}); functoriality follows from the
439	  functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) (identity and composition laws
440	  by \(\otimes\)-induction).
441	\end{definition}
442	
443	\begin{definition}\leanok
444	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
445	  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
446	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
447	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
448	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
449	  assignment
450	  \[
451	    U \;\longmapsto\;
452	      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
453	        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
454	  \]
455	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
456	  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
457	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
458	  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
459	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
460	  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
461	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
462	  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
463	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
464	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
465	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, its codomain row being
466	  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
467	  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
468	  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
469	  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
470	  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
471	\end{definition}
472	
473	\begin{definition}\leanok
474	[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
475	  \label{def:relTensorActL}
476	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
477	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
478	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
479	  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
480	  \[
481	    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
482	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
483	    \;\longrightarrow\;
484	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
485	  \]
486	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
487	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
488	  open \(U\), the additive map
489	  \[
490	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
491	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
492	    \longrightarrow
493	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
494	    \qquad
495	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
496	  \]
497	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
498	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
499	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
500	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
501	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
502	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
503	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
504	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
505	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
506	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
507	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
508	\end{definition}
509	
510	\begin{definition}\leanok
511	[Right-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
512	  \label{def:relTensorActR}
513	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActR}
514	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
515	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
516	  \emph{right-action} natural transformation
517	  \[
518	    a_R : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
519	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
520	    \;\longrightarrow\;
521	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
522	  \]
523	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
524	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
525	  open \(U\), the additive map
526	  \[
527	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
528	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
529	    \longrightarrow
530	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
531	    \qquad
532	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n),
533	  \]
534	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
535	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
536	  action map \(\mathrm{actRmap}\) of
537	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}, the mirror of the left-action
538	  transformation \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}); together \(a_L\) and \(a_R\)
539	  form the parallel pair whose coequalizer presents the relative tensor presheaf
540	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\). Naturality in \(U\) is the compatibility
541	  of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction of
542	  \(s \cdot n\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (n|_V)\), so \(a_R\) commutes with the
543	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps. The verification
544	  reduces to the \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-semilinearity of the restriction maps of
545	  \(\mathcal{Q}\), checked on elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
546	\end{definition}
547	
548	\begin{definition}
549	
550	\leanok
551	[Projection natural transformation of the coequalizer]
552	  \label{def:relTensorProj}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorProj}
554	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
555	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
556	  \emph{projection} natural transformation
557	  \[
558	    \pi : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
559	      \;\longrightarrow\;
560	      \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
561	  \]
562	  from the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) to
563	  the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the presheaf-level monoidal tensor
564	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\) (Mathlib's
565	  \(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\)) is, on an open \(U\), the
566	  canonical quotient additive map
567	  \[
568	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
569	    \longrightarrow
570	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U),
571	    \qquad
572	    m \otimes n \longmapsto m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n,
573	  \]
574	  the objectwise projection \(\mathrm{projL}\) of
575	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}. It is the cofork of the
576	  relative-tensor coequalizer: the third leg, the cokernel of \(a_L - a_R\)
577	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}, \cref{def:relTensorActR}). The apex is identified
578	  with the objectwise value of the presheaf monoidal product through
579	  \cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}.
580	
581	  \emph{Naturality.} The objectwise square asserts
582	  \[
583	    \pi_V \circ
584	      \bigl(\mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
585	        \mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{Q}}\bigr)
586	    \;=\;
587	    \bigl((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\bigr) \circ \pi_U
588	    \qquad (f : V \to U),
589	  \]
590	  both composites sending \(m \otimes n\) to
591	  \(\bigl(m|_V\bigr) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} \bigl(n|_V\bigr)\). On elementary
592	  tensors the left-hand side reduces by functoriality of the
593	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of restriction maps followed by the defining formula of
594	  \(\mathrm{projL}\); the right-hand side reduces by \(\mathrm{projL}\) followed
595	  by the objectwise restriction formula for the presheaf monoidal product
596	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}, on elementary tensors:
597	  \((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\,(m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n) =
598	  (m|_V) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} (n|_V)\)). The two sides then agree by
599	  \(\otimes\)-induction.
600	
601	\end{definition}
602	
603	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
604	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
605	  % NOTE: Lean decl name pending --- no `\lean{}` pin yet; add when the Lean
606	  % declaration for this whiskered-stalkwise-iso lemma lands.
607	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
608	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
609	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
610	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
611	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
612	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
613	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
614	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{proof}
618	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
619	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
620	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
621	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
622	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
623	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
624	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
625	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
626	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
627	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
628	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
629	\end{proof}

631	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
632	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
633	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
634	  \mathlibok
635	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
636	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
637	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
638	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
639	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
640	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
641	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
642	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
643	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
644	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
645	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
646	\end{lemma}
647	
648	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]\leanok
649	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
651	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
652	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
653	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
654	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
655	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
656	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
657	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
658	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
659	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
660	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
661	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
662	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
663	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
664	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
665	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
666	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
667	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
668	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
669	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
670	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
671	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
672	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
673	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
674	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
675	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
676	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
677	  \[
678	    a_L,\, a_R :
679	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
680	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
681	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
682	  \]
683	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
684	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
685	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
686	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
687	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
688	\end{lemma}
689	
690	\begin{proof}\leanok
691	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
692	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
693	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
694	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
695	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
696	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
697	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
698	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
699	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
700	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
701	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
702	
703	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
704	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
705	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
706	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
707	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
708	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
709	
710	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
711	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
712	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
713	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
714	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
715	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
716	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
717	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
718	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
719	  colimit cofork.
720	
721	\end{proof}
722	
723	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
724	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
725	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
726	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
727	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
728	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
729	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
730	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
731	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
732	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
733	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
734	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
735	  parallel pair
736	  \[
737	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
738	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
739	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
740	  \]
741	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
742	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
743	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
744	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
745	\end{lemma}
746	
747	\begin{proof}
748	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
749	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
750	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
751	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
752	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
753	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
754	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
755	  three steps.
756	
757	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
758	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
759	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
760	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
761	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
762	  \[
763	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
764	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
765	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
766	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
767	  \]
768	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
769	
770	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families are precisely
771	  the natural transformations of presheaves on \(X\)
772	  \[
773	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
774	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
775	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
776	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
777	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
778	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
779	  \]
780	  built in the previous definitions: the parallel pair is the left- and
781	  right-action transformations \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}) and \(a_R\)
782	  (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) from the triple presheaf
783	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
784	  (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}), and the cofork leg is the projection
785	  \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) onto the apex. These are morphisms of
786	  functors \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\); naturality of
787	  each in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the restriction
788	  maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\) (the restriction of
789	  \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection),
790	  recorded in those definitions. Objectwise at each \(U\) the cofork
791	  \((a_L, a_R, \pi)_U\) is the colimit cofork of step 1. Colimits in a functor
792	  category are computed objectwise --- by
793	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
794	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
795	  coequalizers of step 1 promote to a coequalizer of the displayed parallel pair
796	  \(a_L, a_R\) with cofork \(\pi\) in
797	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\).
798	
799	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
800	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
801	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
802	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
803	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
804	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
805	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
806	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
807	  along this identification exhibits
808	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
809	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
810	\end{proof}

812	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
813	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
814	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
815	  \mathlibok
816	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
817	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
818	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
819	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
820	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
821	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
822	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
823	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
824	  category are computed objectwise.
825	\end{lemma}
