845	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
846	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
847	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
848	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
849	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
850	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
851	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
852	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
853	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
854	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
855	  the sheafification adjunction
856	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
857	  presheaves of modules, and write
858	  \[
859	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
860	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
861	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
862	  \]
863	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
864	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
865	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
866	  \[
867	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
868	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
869	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
870	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
871	  \]
872	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
873	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
874	\end{lemma}
875	
876	\begin{proof}
877	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
878	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
879	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
880	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
881	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
882	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
883	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
884	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
885	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
886	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
887	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
888	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
889	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
890	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
891	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
892	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
893	
894	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
895	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
896	  is the objectwise relative tensor
897	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
898	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
899	  maps
900	  \[
901	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
902	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
903	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
904	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
905	  \]
906	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
907	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
908	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
909	  pair
910	  \[
911	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
912	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
913	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
914	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
915	  \]
916	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
917	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
918	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
919	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
920	
921	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
922	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
923	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
924	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
925	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
926	  relating the rows,
927	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
928	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
929	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
930	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).
931	
932	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
933	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
934	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
935	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
936	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
937	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
938	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
939	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
940	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
941	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
942	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
943	  Under this identification the stalk of
944	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
945	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
946	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
947	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
948	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
949	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
950	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
951	
952	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
953	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
954	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
955	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
956	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
957	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
958	\end{proof}

969	\begin{lemma}[Cramer-inverse cocycle on the triple overlap]
970	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}
971	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_matrix}
972	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
973	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
974	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
975	  Over the common triple-overlap ring (the localisation carrying the three charts'
976	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
977	  multiplicative cocycle identity
978	  \[
979	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
980	  \]
981	  This is the pure matrix-algebra core of (C2), independent of any sheaf data.
982	\end{lemma}
983	\begin{proof}
984	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
985	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
986	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
987	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
988	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
989	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column
990	  submatrix commutes with left multiplication (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}),
991	  \[
992	    X^J_K \;=\; \bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_K .
993	  \]
994	  Both \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\) have unit determinant
995	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so this product of invertibles inverts
996	  by \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\):
997	  \[
998	    (X^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J).
999	  \]
1000	  Multiplying on the right by \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) and cancelling \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1\)
1001	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the
1002	  cancellation packaged in \cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}) gives
1003	  \((X^J_K)^{-1} (X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\). The agreement of the three image
1004	  matrices over the triple overlap that licenses these manipulations is the content of
1005	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}, the matrix form of the scheme-level cocycle
1006	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}.
1007	\end{proof}

1019	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) and the endpoint bridges

1021	  matrix automorphism of the corresponding base-changed Cramer inverse on the common

1036	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}\) of a localised Cramer inverse, conjugated by the
1037	  free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} on its source and target.
1038	  Transporting to \(V_{IJK}\) along the relevant projection or triple transition by
1039	  \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} reassociates the iterated pullbacks
1040	  through the pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}; the resulting
1041	  source, middle, and target sheaves are pinned to a single free sheaf
1042	  \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\) by the three endpoint bridges
1043	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}: the \emph{src} bridge identifies the common source of
1044	  \(\widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\), the \emph{mid} bridge the
1045	  target of \(\widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}\) with the source of \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I}\),
1046	  and the \emph{tgt} bridge the target of \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I}\) with that of
1047	  \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\). Under these identifications the free-pullback comparisons
1048	  cancel and each transported transition becomes
1049	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\) of the corresponding base-changed Cramer inverse:
1050	  \(\widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K} \mapsto \mathrm{matrixEnd}((X^I_J)^{-1})\),
1051	  \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \mapsto \mathrm{matrixEnd}((X^J_K)^{-1})\),
1052	  \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J} \mapsto \mathrm{matrixEnd}((X^I_K)^{-1})\). By the linkage
1053	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} the composite of the first two is
1054	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\bigl((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\), and the matrix identity
1055	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} rewrites the argument as \((X^I_K)^{-1}\), so the
1056	  composite equals \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\). The bridges guarantee both sides are
1057	  isomorphisms between the same pair of sheaves on \(V_{IJK}\), so the equality
1058	  is well-formed.
1059	\end{proof}
1060	
1061	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1062	[Triple-overlap multiplicativity of the bundle transition (C2)]
1063	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}
1064	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle}
1065	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1066	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport, lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport,
1067	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1068	  Over each triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\) the base-change transports
1069	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three bundle transitions satisfy
1070	  \[
1071	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1072	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J},
1073	  \]
1074	  the module-level form of the descent condition (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for
1075	  the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\). This is the \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued multiplicative cocycle
1076	  of \(\mathcal{U}\).
1077	\end{lemma}
1078	\begin{proof}
1079	  \uses{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1080	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1081	  The statement decomposes into three independent steps. First, the matrix-algebra core
1082	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} establishes the Cramer-inverse cocycle
1083	  \((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the triple overlap. Second, the linkage
1084	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} records that composition of matrix automorphisms realises
1085	  matrix multiplication. Third, the transport step \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1086	  carries the three transitions to the common overlap \(V_{IJK}\), aligns their source,
1087	  middle, and target sheaves through the base-change transport and the endpoint bridges, and
1088	  -- feeding it the matrix identity through the linkage -- yields exactly the stated equality
1089	  \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K} = \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\).
1090	\end{proof}

1093	
1094	\leanok
1095	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
1096	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
1097	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
1098	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
1099	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
1100	    def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, def:gr_bundleTransition,
1101	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul,
1102	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1103	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1104	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1105	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
1106	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1107	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
1108	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
1109	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
1110	  %  by putting
1111	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
1112	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1113	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
1114	  \[
1115	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
1116	  \]
1117	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
1118	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
1119	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
1120	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
1121	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
1122	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
1123	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
1124	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
1125	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
1126	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
1127	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
1128	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
1129	\end{definition}

1131	\begin{definition}
1132	
1133	\leanok
1134	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
1135	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
1136	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
1137	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1138	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
1139	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1140	    def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1141	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1142	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1143	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1145	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
1146	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
1147	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1148	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1149	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1150	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1151	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1152	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1153	  \[
1154	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1155	  \]
1156	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1157	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
1158	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1159	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1160	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1161	  \[
1162	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1163	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1164	  \]
1165	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1166	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1167	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1168	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1169	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1170	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1171	\end{definition}

1173	\section{The functor of points}
1174	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1175	
1176	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1177	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1178	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1179	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1180	identities that make that action functorial.
1181	
1182	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1183	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1184	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1185	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1186	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1187	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1188	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1189	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1190	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1191	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1192	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1193	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1194	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1195	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1196	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1197	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1198	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1199	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1200	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1201	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1202	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1203	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1204	  functor's value at \(T\).
1205	
1206	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1207	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1208	  \[
1209	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1210	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1211	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1212	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1213	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1214	  \]
1215	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1216	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1217	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1218	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1219	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1220	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1221	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1222	  map of equivalence classes.
1223	\end{definition}
1224	
1225	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1226	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1227	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1228	
1229	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1230	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1231	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1233	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1234	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1235	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1236	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1237	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1238	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1239	\end{lemma}
1240	
1241	\begin{proof}
1242	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1243	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1244	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1245	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1246	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1247	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1248	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1249	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1250	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1251	\end{proof}
1252	
1253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1254	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1255	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1257	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1258	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1259	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1260	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1261	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1262	\end{lemma}
1263	
1264	\begin{proof}
1265	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1266	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1267	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1268	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1269	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1270	\end{proof}
1271	
1272	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1273	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1274	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1276	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1277	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1278	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1279	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1280	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1281	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
1282	\end{lemma}
1283	
1284	\begin{proof}
1285	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1286	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1287	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1288	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1289	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1290	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1291	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1292	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1293	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1294	  well-defined.
1295	\end{proof}
1296	
1297	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1298	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1299	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1300	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1301	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1302	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1303	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1304	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1305	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1306	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1307	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1308	\end{lemma}
1309	
1310	\begin{proof}
1311	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1312	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1313	  hom-set bijections.
1314	\end{proof}
1315	
1316	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1317	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1318	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1320	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1321	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1322	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1323	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1324	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1325	\end{lemma}
1326	
1327	\begin{proof}
1328	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1329	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1330	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1331	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
1332	  tracked through each pullback).
1333	\end{proof}
1334	
1335	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1336	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1337	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1338	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1339	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1340	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1341	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
1342	
1343	\begin{definition}
1344	
1345	\leanok
1346	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1347	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1349	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1350	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1351	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1352	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1353	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1354	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1355	  % "represents the
1356	  %  contravariant functor
1357	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1358	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1359	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1360	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1361	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1362	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1363	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1364	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1365	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1366	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1367	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1368	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1369	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1370	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1371	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1372	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1373	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1374	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1375	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1376	  \[
1377	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1378	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1379	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1380	  \]
1381	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1382	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1383	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1384	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1385	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1386	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1387	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1388	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1389	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1390	\end{definition}
1391	
1392	\section{The universal property}
1393	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1394	
1395	\begin{theorem}
1396	
1397	\leanok
1398	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1399	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1401	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1402	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1403	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1405	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1408	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1409	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1410	  %  contravariant functor
1411	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1412	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1413	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1414	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1415	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1416	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1417	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1418	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1419	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1420	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1421	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1422	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1423	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1424	  \[
1425	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1426	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1427	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1428	    \qquad
1429	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1430	      \;\longmapsto\;
1431	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1432	  \]
1433	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1434	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1435	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1436	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1437	  universal family.
1438	\end{theorem}
1439	
1440	\begin{proof}
1441	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1442	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1443	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1444	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1445	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1446	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1447	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1448	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1449	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1450	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1451	
1452	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1453	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1454	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1455	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1456	  the composite
1457	  \[
1458	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1459	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1460	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1461	  \]
1462	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1463	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1464	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1465	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1466	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1467	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1468	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1469	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1470	
1471	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1472	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1473	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1474	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1475	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1476	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1477	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1478	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1479	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1480	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1481	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1482	
1483	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1484	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1485	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1486	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1487	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1488	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1489	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1490	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1491	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1492	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1493	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1494	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1495	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1496	
1497	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1498	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1499	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1500	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1501	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1502	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1503	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1504	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1505	
1506	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1507	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1508	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1509	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1510	\end{proof}
1511

1031	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
1032	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
1033	  \]
1034	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1035	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
1036	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
1037	\end{proof}

1039	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
1040	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1041	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
1042	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1043	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1044	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1045	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1046	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1047	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1048	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1049	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
1050	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
1051	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1052	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1053	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1054	  power isomorphisms).}
1055	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1056	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1057	  \[
1058	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1059	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1060	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1061	  \]
1062	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1063	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1064	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1065	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1066	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1067	\end{lemma}
1068	
1069	\begin{proof}
1070	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1071	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1072	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1073	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1074	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1075	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1076	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1077	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1078	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1079	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1080	
1081	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1082	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1083	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1084	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1085	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1086	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1087	  sheaf tensor products
1088	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1089	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1090	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1091	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1092	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1093	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1094	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1095	  of the tensor product is never used.
1096	
1097	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1098	  \(m = 0\),
1099	  \[
1100	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1101	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1102	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1103	  \]
1104	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1105	  recursive definition
1106	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1107	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1108	  \[
1109	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1110	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1111	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1112	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1113	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1114	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1115	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1116	  \]
1117	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1118	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1119	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1120	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1121	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1122	  \[
1123	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1124	      \mathcal{L}
1125	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1126	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1127	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1128	  \]
1129	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1130	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1131	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1132	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1133	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1134	
1135	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1136	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1137	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1138	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1139	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1140	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1141	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1142	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1143	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1144	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1145	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1146	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1147	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1148	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1149	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1150	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1151	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1152	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1153	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1154	\end{proof}

1156	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1157	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1158	
1159	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1160	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1161	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1162	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1163	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1164	global sections.
1165	
1166	\begin{definition}\leanok
1167	[Section multiplication]
1168	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1169	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1170	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1171	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1172	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1173	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1174	  \[
1175	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1176	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1177	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1178	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1179	  \]
1180	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1181	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1182	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1183	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1184	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1185	  the sheafification unit
1186	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1187	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1188	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1189	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1190	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1191	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1192	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1193	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1194	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1195	\end{definition}
1196	
1197	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1198	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1200	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1201	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1202	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1203	  the two composites
1204	  \[
1205	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1206	    \longrightarrow
1207	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1208	    \cong
1209	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1210	  \]
1211	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1212	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1213	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1214	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1215	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1216	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1217	\end{lemma}
1218	
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1221	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1222	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1223	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1224	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1225	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1226	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1227	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1228	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1229	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1230	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1231	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1232	  section multiplications.
1233	\end{proof}

1235	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1236	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1237	
1238	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1239	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1240	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1241	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1242	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1243	
1244	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1245	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1246	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1247	  \mathlibok
1248	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1249	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1250	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1251	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1252	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1253	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1254	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1255	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1256	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1257	\end{lemma}
1258	
1259	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1260	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1261	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1262	  \mathlibok
1263	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1264	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1265	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1266	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1267	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1268	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1269	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1270	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1271	  extension of the graded action.
1272	\end{lemma}
1273	
1274	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1275	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1277	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1278	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
1279	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1280	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1281	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1282	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1283	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1284	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1285	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1286	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1287	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1288	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
1289	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
1290	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
1291	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
1292	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1293	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1294	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1295	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1296	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1297	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1298	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1299	  multiplication is the composite
1300	  \[
1301	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1302	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1303	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1304	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1305	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1306	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1307	  \]
1308	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1309	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1310	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1311	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1312	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1313	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1314	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1315	\end{lemma}
1316	
1317	\begin{proof}
1318	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
1319	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1320	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
1321	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
1322	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
1323	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
1324	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
1325	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
1326	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
1327	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
1328	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1329	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
1330	  commutative-semiring structure.
1331	\end{proof}
1332	
1333	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
1334	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
1335	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
1336	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1337	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
1338	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1339	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1340	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1341	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
1342	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1343	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
1344	  %  $$
1345	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
1346	  %  =
1347	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
1348	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
1349	  %  $$
1350	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
1351	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1352	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
1353	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
1354	  \(X_s\). The family
1355	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
1356	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
1357	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
1358	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
1359	  action
1360	  \[
1361	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1362	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
1363	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1364	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
1365	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
1366	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1367	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1368	  \]
1369	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
1370	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
1371	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
1372	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
1373	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
1374	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
1375	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
1376	\end{lemma}
1377	
1378	\begin{proof}
1379	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
1380	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1381	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
1382	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
1383	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
1384	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
1385	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
1386	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
1387	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
1388	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
1389	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
1390	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
1391	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
1392	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}

73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements

76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.

96	
97

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57

71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)

79	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
80	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80

2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,

13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179

77	% against it.

**Target**: C2 chain L1–L3 + `lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul`

5. `def:gr_bundleTransitionData` (`lean{bundleTransitionData}`) — pure packaging def, `\uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, def:scheme_modules_glue}`. ✓
All five: no Lean tactic leakage, correctly `\uses{}`-linked, faithful to `\lean{}` targets. Not in `unmatched_lean` (sorry stubs exist). ✓
